description: Cross-Site ETag Length Leak (PortSwigger Top 10 / 2025, #6 — Kaneko). The ETag header is a strong validator whose length / format encodes response size or content hash. When ETag is exposed to cross-origin requests (no-cors fetch, img/audio/video request), an attacker measures ETag bytes via cache or oracle to leak per-victim response size — yielding XS-leak of state (logged-in vs out, search-result count, message-count, account-balance bucket).

etag_length_oracle.description: Strong ETag whose length varies with response size or contains state-bearing hash. Cross-origin fetch with `no-cors` reads ETag from the cached entry indirectly via 304 round-trip timing.

probes.description: Fetch the endpoint with no Authorization, capture ETag length. Fetch with valid session, capture ETag length. Length delta = state leak; format change (W/ prefix vs strong) = state oracle.

variables.note: Compare authenticated vs anonymous ETag for the same URL.

probes.description: Cross-origin XS-leak — issue If-None-Match with attacker-chosen ETag. 304 response means ETag matched (state confirmed); 200 means it changed. Pair across N candidates to bisect leaked state.

etag_hash_disclosure.description: ETag computed as hash of response body. When the body contains per-user secrets (token, balance, role), the ETag becomes a side-channel for those values via hash collision tables or chosen-prefix attacks.

probes.description: Endpoint that returns per-user JSON with stable ETag — ETag value differs per user. Combined with weak hash (CRC32, MD5 truncated), ETag enables enumeration.
